PREAMB.SUBJECT: Energy Conservation Program: Definition of Showerhead

AGY.P: Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy, Department of Energy.

SUM.P: On July 22, 2021, the U.S. Department of Energy (âDOEâ) published a notice of proposed rulemaking (âNOPRâ) to revise the current definition of âshowerheadâ adopted in the December 16, 2020, final rule (âDecember 2020 Final Ruleâ) by reinstating the October 2013 definition of âshowerhead,â withdraw the December 2020 final rule's interpretation of the term âshowerhead,â and withdraw the associated definition for âbody spray.â DOE did not propose any changes to the definition of âsafety shower showerhead.â In this final rule, DOE revises the current definition of âshowerheadâ adopted in the December 2020 final rule by reinstating the October 2013 definition of âshowerheadâ as the Department finds that it is more consistent with the purposes of the Energy Policy and Conservation Act, as amended (âEPCAâ). In addition, DOE removes the current definition of âbody sprayâ adopted in the December 16, 2020 final rule. Finally, DOE maintains the definition of âsafety shower showerheadâ adopted in the December 2020 final rule.

DATES.P: The effective date of this rule is January 19, 2022.

ADD.P: The docket for this activity, which includes Federal Register notices, comments, and other supporting documents/materials, is available for review at www.regulations.gov. All documents in the docket are listed in the www.regulations.gov index. However, not all documents listed in the index may be publicly available, such as information that is exempt from public disclosure.

The docket web page can be found at www.regulations.gov/docket/EERE-2021-BT-STD-0016. The docket web page contains instructions on how to access all documents, including public comments, in the docket.
For further information on how to review the docket, contact the Appliance and Equipment Standards Program staff at (202) 287-1445 or by email: ApplianceStandardsQuestions@ee.doe.gov.

SUPLINF.HD: I. Introduction
SUPLINF.P: The following section briefly discusses the statutory authority underlying this final rule, as well as the relevant historical background related to showerheads, the subject of this final rule.
SUPLINF.HD: A. Authority
SUPLINF.P:
Title III of Energy Policy and Conservation Act, as amended (âEPCAâ), (42 U.S.C. 6291 et seq. ) sets forth a variety of provisions designed to improve energy efficiency and, for certain products, water efficiency. 1
Part B of Title IIIâ 2
establishes the âEnergy Conservation Program for Consumer Products Other Than Automobiles,â which includes showerheads (with the exception of safety shower showerheads)âthe subject of this rulemaking. (42 U.S.C. 6292(a)(15)) Under EPCA, the energy conservation program consists essentially of four parts: (1) Testing, (2) labeling, (3) Federal energy conservation standards, and (4) certification and enforcement procedures.

FTNT.P: 1 âAll references to EPCA in this final rule refer to the statute as amended through the Energy Act of 2020, Public Law 116-260 (Dec. 27, 2020).
2 âFor editorial reasons, upon codification in the U.S. Code, Part B was redesignated as Part A.

SUPLINF.HD: B. Background
SUPLINF.P:
EPCA defines a showerhead as âany showerhead (including a handheld showerhead), except a safety shower showerhead.â (42 U.S.C. 6291(31)(D)) In addition to defining âshowerhead,â Congress established a maximum water use threshold of 2.5 gpm applicable to âany showerhead.â (42 U.S.C. 6295(j)(1)). The definition of âshowerheadâ and the water conservation standard for showerheads were added to EPCA by the Energy
Policy Act of 1992 (Pub. L. 102-486 (Oct. 24, 1992)) (âEPAct 1992â).

Until 2013, DOE regulations did not contain a separate definition for âshowerhead.â ( See 78 FR 62970) (Oct. 23, 2013) On May 19, 2010, DOE published in the Federal Register a Notice of Availability of a proposed interpretive rule regarding the definition of âshowerhead.â 75 FR 27926 (â2010 Draft Interpretive Ruleâ) In the 2010 Draft Interpretive Rule, 3
DOE discussed how there was uncertainty about how the EPCA definition of âshowerheadâ applies to the diversified showerhead product offerings. Id. at 1. To address this uncertainty, DOE proposed to define a âshowerheadâ as âany plumbing fitting that is designed to direct water onto a bather.â Id. at 2 (footnote omitted). As such, DOE stated it would âfind a showerhead to be noncompliant with EPCA's maximum water use standard if the showerhead's standard components, operating in their maximum design flow configuration, taken together use in excess of 2.5 gpm.â Id. at 3.

On March 4, 2011, DOE formally withdrew the draft interpretive rule and issued showerhead enforcement guidance. 4
(â2011 Enforcement Guidanceâ) In the 2011 Enforcement Guidance, DOE explained that it had received several complaints alleging that certain showerhead products exceeded EPCA's 2.5 gpm standard. DOE stated that it had learned that some had come to believe that a showerhead that expels water from multiple nozzles constituted not a single showerhead, but rather multiple showerheads and thus could exceed the maximum permitted water use by a multiple equal to the number of nozzles on the showerhead. Id. at 1. Following a review of the record from the 2010 Draft Interpretive Rule, DOE concluded that the term âany showerheadâ has been and continues to be sufficiently clear such that no interpretive rule was needed. Id. at 2. Specifically, DOE stated that âmultiple spraying components sold together as a single unit designed to spray water onto a single bather constitutes a single showerhead for the purpose of the maximum water use standard.â Id. DOE used its discretion and addressed the misunderstanding of how to measure compliance with the standard by providing a two-year enforcement grace period to allow manufacturers to sell any remaining noncompliant products. Id. at 2-3.

On May 30, 2012, DOE proposed to revise the test procedure for showerheads and other products and to change the regulatory definition of showerheads. 77 FR 31742 (âMay 2012 NOPRâ). DOE proposed to adopt definitions for four terms related to showerheadsââfittingâ, âaccessoryâ, âbody sprayâ, and âshowerheadââin order to address certain provisions of the revised American Society of Mechanical Engineers/American National Standards Institute (âASME/ANSIâ) test procedures that were not contemplated in the versions referenced by the existing DOE test procedure, and to establish greater clarity with respect to product coverage. 77 FR 31742, 31747. 5
Specifically, DOE proposed to define âshowerheadâ as âan accessory, or set of accessories, to a supply fitting distributed in commerce for attachment to a single supply fitting, for spraying water onto a bather, typically from an overhead position, including body sprays and hand-held showerheads, but excluding safety shower showerheads.â 77 FR 31742. 31755. The proposed definition clarified that DOE considered a âbody sprayâ to be a showerhead for the purposes of regulatory coverage. 77 FR 31742, 31747.

FTNT.P: 5 âDOE also proposed to adopt a definition for âhand-held showerheadâ in the May 2012 NOPR. 77 FR 31742, 31747. This final rule does not reference that discussion, as DOE is not proposing any edits to the existing definition of âhand-held showerhead.â

SUPLINF.P: Responding to comments on the May 2012 NOPR, DOE issued on April 8, 2013 a supplemental notice of proposed rulemaking (âSNOPRâ) in which DOE proposed a revised definition of âshowerheadâ and withdrew its proposal to include âbody spraysâ in the definition of âshowerheadâ in light of concerns raised by commenters and DOE's need to further study the issue. 78 FR 20832, 20834-20835, 20841 (âApril 2013 SNOPRâ). The SNOPR's modified definition of âshowerheadâ removed the term âaccessoryâ from the definition based on comments about the use of the term. 78 FR 20832, 20834. Under the proposed modified definition, a âshowerheadâ is âa component of a supply fitting, or set of components distributed in commerce for attachment to a single supply fitting, for spraying water onto a bather, typically from an overhead position, including hand-held showerheads, but excluding safety shower showerheads.â 78 FR 20832, 20834. DOE also requested comment on whether to define the term âsafety shower showerheadâ to address which products qualify for exclusion from coverage under EPCA and DOE regulations. 78 FR 20832, 20835, 20840.

On October 23, 2013, DOE issued a final rule amending test procedures for showerheads and other products and adopting definitions for products, including showerheads. 78 FR 62970 (âOctober 2013 Final Ruleâ). In this final rule, DOE adopted in substance the modified definition of âshowerheadâ proposed in the April 2013 SNOPR. 78 FR 62970, 62986. The October 2013 Final Rule defined âshowerheadâ as âa component or set of components distributed in commerce for attachment to a single supply fitting, for spraying water onto a bather, typically from an overhead position, excluding safety shower showerheads.â Id. at 78 FR 62986. DOE did not finalize the definition of âbody sprayâ proposed in the May 2012 NOPR. Id. at 78 FR 62973. DOE also declined to adopt a definition of âsafety shower showerheadâ, and explained that it was unable to identify a definition that would clearly distinguish these products from the showerheads covered under EPCA. Id. at 78 FR 62974.

On August 13, 2020, DOE proposed revising the definition of a âshowerheadâ to be consistent with the most recent ASME standard. 85 FR 49284 (âAugust 2020 NOPRâ). DOE also proposed to adopt definitions of âbody sprayâ and âsafety shower showerheadâ and to clarify whether the current test procedure would apply to the proposed definitional changes. Id. at 85 FR 49285. In addition, DOE proposed to amend the test procedure for showerheads to address the testing of a single showerhead within a multiheaded showerhead. Id. at 85 FR 49292.

On December 16, 2020, DOE published a final rule amending the definition of âshowerheadâ and adopting definitions for âbody sprayâ and âsafety shower showerhead.â 85 FR 81341. Specifically, the December 2020 Final Rule amended the meaning of âshowerheadâ to restate the statutory definition and explicitly define the term through incorporation of the ASME definition to mean âan accessory to a supply fitting for spraying onto a bather, typically from an overhead position.â Id. at 85 FR 81342, 81359. In the December 2020 Final Rule's definition, DOE interpreted the new definition to mean that each âshowerheadâ included in a product with multiple showerheads would be considered separately for purposes of determining standards compliance. Id. at 85 FR 81342. In addition, DOE established a definition for âbody sprayâ, citing the need to address ambiguity about whether body sprays were considered showerheads
under the October 2013 Final Rule. Id. at 85 FR 81342, 81350. DOE defined the term âbody sprayâ as âa shower device for spraying water onto a bather from other than the overhead position. A body spray is not a showerhead.â Id. at 85 FR 81359. Lastly, DOE defined the term âsafety shower showerheadâ by incorporating by reference the definition of âsafety shower showerheadâ from the ANSI/International Safety Equipment Association (âISEAâ) Z358.1-2014, 6
such that a âsafety shower showerheadâ is âa showerhead designed to meet the requirements of ISEA Z358.1.â Id. at 85 FR 81359. The December 2020 Final Rule determined that leaving the term âsafety shower showerheadâ undefined would cause confusion as to which products are excluded from the definition of âshowerhead.â Id. at 85 FR 81351. DOE did not finalize the test procedure amendments that had been proposed in the August 2020 NOPR. Id. at 85 FR 81351.

FTNT.P: 6 âANSI/ISEA Z358.1-2014, âAmerican National Standard for Emergency Eyewash and Shower Equipment.â

SUPLINF.P: On January 20, 2021, the President issued Executive Order 13990, âProtecting Public Health and the Environment and Restoring Science to Tackle the Climate Crisis.â 86 FR 7037 (Jan. 25, 2021) (âE.O. 13990â). Section 1 of that Order lists a number of policies related to the protection of public health and the environment, including reducing greenhouse gas (âGHGâ) emissions and bolstering the Nation's resilience to the impacts of climate change. Id. at 86 FR 7041. Section 2 of the Order instructs all agencies to review âexisting regulations, orders, guidance documents, policies, and any other similar agency actions promulgated, issued, or adopted between January 20, 2017, and January 20, 2021, that are or may be inconsistent with, or present obstacles to, [these policies].â Id. Agencies are directed, as appropriate and consistent with applicable law, to consider suspending, revising, or rescinding these agency actions. Id.

While E.O. 13990 triggered the Department's re-evaluation of the December 2020 Final Rule, DOE relies upon the analysis presented below, based upon EPCA, to revise the definition âshowerheadâ and to withdraw the definition of âbody sprayâ in the July 2021 NOPR and in this final rule. On July 22, 2021, DOE issued a NOPR (âJuly 2021 NOPRâ) in which it proposed to revise the definition of âshowerheadâ adopted in the December 2020 Final Rule by reinstating the prior definition of âshowerhead.â 86 FR 38594. Further, DOE tentatively determined that, in reinstating the prior definition of âshowerhead,â all components attached to a single supply fitting ( i.e., all nozzles or spraying components within a product containing multiple nozzles or spraying components) would be considered part of a single showerhead for determining compliance with the 2.5 gpm standard. Id. In addition, DOE proposed to withdraw the current definition of âbody sprayâ adopted in the December 2020 Final Rule. Id. Finally, DOE did not propose any changes to the definition of âsafety shower showerheadâ adopted in the December 2020 Final Rule. Id.

DOE invited comment on all aspects of July 2021 NOPR, including data and information to assist in evaluating whether the definition of âshowerheadâ from the October 2013 Final Rule should be reinstated. Id. at 86 FR 38594. On August 31, 2021, DOE held a webinar to present the substance of the July 2021 NOPR and afford interested parties an opportunity to present comments. 7

FTNT.P: 7 âThe webinar presentation and transcript are available in the docket at www.regulations.gov/docket/EERE-2021-BT-STD-0016/document.

SUPLINF.P: DOE received comments in response to the July 2021 NOPR from the interested parties listed in Table I.1.DOE received two comments that were not within the scope of the rulemaking. 8
